sentences.text: Well, the-- I have one-- one funny experience to tell you.
I didn't want to come here.
But my father insisted that we came here.
So of course, we did.
So where we-- they put us up on Lafayette Street, which is near Astor Place.
And on the first day that I was there, I went outside.
I wanted to look at America.
And I walked-- I made a right turn at the street.
And I walked over to the Bowery.
And I saw all these bums lying there in the streets, soiling themselves, and all these winos.
And I went back to-- to-- to the-- to the HIAS building.
And I said to my father, what have you done to us?
The people sleep in the street.
They are dirty.
They are disgusting.
How can we live here?
Well, then I found out later that it was the Bowery, and it was all right.
Well, I-- we got a room-- we got a room from the Joint Distribution Committee.
We got an apartment someplace.
We stayed in-- in that place, on Astor Place, we had beds there for about four weeks.
Then they found us an apartment.
They found us two furnished rooms out in Brooklyn someplace, with a Jewish woman.
And we went out there.
And they found me a job as an alteration hand.
They asked me, can I sew?
And I said yes, I can sew.
They found me a job in a dress shop, as an-- altering, women.
So I worked there for four weeks.
I guess I wasn't fast enough or whatever.
And I worked someplace else, and making underwear and things.
I worked on a sewing machine for a while.
And-- and-- and-- and then I met a girl.
And she said she was working for neckties, and they were paying much better than the underwear people.
So I asked her if she could get me a job.
And she said, yes.
And by the time, I could run an electric sewing machine.
Because I had learned in the other bad places.
So when I came there-- and I'm a very neat person.
I have good hands.
I can sew nicely, and things like that.
But that was speed.
So to make a long story short, there was piecework.
And we worked on piecework.
And I-- and I figured out that the people were-- if you-- if you do it faster, you can do more.
And if you do more, you get paid better.
So I worked out a-- a system where I could do.
And other people were making 30 and-- and 35 dollars a week.
Some of them were making $28.
I was making $125, $135.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So one day, my boss called me in.
And he wanted to know how it was possible that I made so much more money than other people.
He says, we inspect your work.
It is perfectly satisfactory.
How can you make them?
I says, I figured out how to make no unnecessary movement.
I said, besides which, I'm hungrier.
I need it more.
I have a motive-- motivation from that.
So he wanted to know what I was doing.
And I told him I was going to school at night and taking courses.
And he said, all right.
Really?
And yeah.
And he said, well, the-- the-- the-- well, anyway, but I was making a lot of money.
He wanted me to work in the office.
But they were only paying $50 or $60 at that time, for a bookkeeper.
And I was making $125.
And a guy that worked in an advertising agency on Madison Avenue, they had an article in 'The Post.' He was making $111 a week.
So my husband-- I met-- I met my husband.
And we-- we-- we-- he was from Vienna too.
And we got married.
His-- his-- his name is Bert, Bert Manning.
He was from Vienna too.
He came to this-- he came to this country in-- in-- 1939.
And he was in the American Army.
And he was in CID at the end of the war, in-- in-- in-- in the army.
He was interrogating German prisoners and things like that.
And then he came back.
And he didn't have a profession, because before the war he had been working as a sheet metal worker.
And one day he fell.
And he was saved by this much, but not-- he fell off of a-- a scaffolding-- from not smashing his head into-- into a concrete sidewalk.
So he didn't want to go back to that kind of business, even though it was well-paying job.
So we decided-- we decided I was going to go to work.
I was making enough money to carry us both.
And he was going to go to school.
So he took-- he went for engineering.
And he did-- he did it in 23 months.
He went-- went 11-- he went for 11 months, and then another year later.
He took a month off in between.
And he took the whole course in 23 months.
He was working, you know?
Like, he was taking double and triple things, everything.
And he had been in the engineers before he went into the intelligence.
So he knew a little bit about it.
So he-- he went-- so we got married.
And for the first two years, I carried him.
Then after he got-- he got-- well, when he came out of school, it was 1950.
And it was very difficult to find a job, because of the Korean War.
And there was not enough concrete, and not building materials.
Anyway, so he had to work.
The first job he had, he didn't get anything.
He was working for the experience.
They didn't pay him anything.
And then he found another job.
And then I realized I could-- I didn't have to sew on the machine anymore.
So I offered my boss that I-- I could work in the office now.
So I was made the bookkeeper.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I watched what everybody was doing.
And I'm quick on a-- a quick study, and on the uptake.
So I was a bookkeeper.
And I worked there for-- for about a year or so.
And then I left.
And I went someplace else.
And by that time, I was an experienced bookkeeper.
And-- and I had my own department.
And I was running it.
You know, I was the only one they had.
And I was very good.
And-- and then I broke-- then I broke my leg skiing once, on a winter-- my husband worked in construction.
So we did take our vacations in the winter.
And he was a skiing enthusiast.
So I became a skier.
And we went skiing.
And that's how we did our vacation.
And one year, I broke my leg.
So I had to stay home.
And they couldn't keep the job open.
They had to have somebody to run the department.
So then I worked someplace else.
And by that time, I was already-- by that time I was already a bookkeeper for-- for-- I had three people working under me.
And then I stayed home.
And then I became pregnant.
I had a few miscarriages.
But then I finally became pregnant.
And I had a baby.
It was Gregory.
He was born in April 27, 1957.
And I was home for-- and then I had another child, in 1960, my daughter, Pamela.
She was born on July 24th, 1960.
And I was home until 1968. 8:00 And then my marriage started to break apart.
And I decided that I was going to go back to work.
And when I went around, they gave me tests, and decided that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --I was overqualified for the job.
I went to [INAUDIBLE].
I was going to become a margin clerk.
And they said, in four weeks I was going to tell her, the-- my boss what to do, and they didn't want me.
Because I came out too high on the test.
You know, the aptitude test they were giving.
And the phone company, the woman that tested me, she said-- she said, don't work here.
She says, this is not a job for you.
She says, you're the first person that I know that has completed the test in under a half hour, and no-- no answers wrong.
And most people take an hour and a half.
She says, you don't want to work here.
So I was looking.
And my sister-in-law kept saying, before you can walk, you have to learn how to crawl.
And I says, I'm not crawling anywheres.
I decided to become an accountant.
I went out and I bought myself a book called for $2.25.
And I became an accountant.
And the next shop I applied to was for a-- a management agency.
It was called [?
Kogen and Bell ?].
They were located in the Empire State Building.
Oh, by that time, my marriage was-- [?
I ?] [? think, ?] and I decided to go back to work.
But my husband was still here.
But it was in the process of being dissolved.
And I went to there-- there.
And I was a full-charge bookkeeper.
And they hired me.
And I was managing people's accounts.
You know, like, paying their bills and being the coolie.
Mr.
[INAUDIBLE] is a very crude, horrible man, who was very-- spoke to me only in four-letter words.
I mean, I know-- I know-- Anne Bancroft, I did her work.
And I did Peggy Cass.
We managed quite a few well-known people.
My boss at that time was-- was-- he was a theatrical producer.
He produced 'Hair,' and-- and-- and things like that.
So I was involved in it.
I stayed there.
And I left there a year later.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mr.
[INAUDIBLE], one day told me what I could do to [? my ?] he told me to do something.
And I said, if I can do it to me, you can do it to you.
And my boss threw me out.
[LAUGHS] Anyway, that's how I lost my job in the management, in the personal management thing.
The next thing, I went in with the real estate company.
And I stayed there.
And I was looking for a better job.
And I got a job, that I pay-- that I got about $70 a week more.
And they liked me.
And when they called my boss for references, he says, yeah, she's very smart.
But you can't depend on her.
If she doesn't feel like coming in, she doesn't show up.
Because he-- he didn't want to lose me.
Because I was running the whole show.
I was-- I was very good.
As soon as I found out, I went in and I said, I quit.
He says, I'll never give you another reference again.
I said, what you did to me-- he says-- you know, I asked him for a raise.
He says, this is not a profit-making corporation.
I says, well I am not a charitable institution.
I can't work for nothing.
Anyway, any-- so when I found out that he had done this to me, I just-- he says-- he says, never mind.
He says, I'll never give you another reference.
I says, nobody's ever going to ask you.
You're going to disappear from the face of the earth.
And I went out right then, at 11:30.
And I went out.
And I said, I'm going out now for a while.
And I'm going to come back.
And then I'm going to take my things and I'm going to go home.
He says, think about it.
Go out for an hour.
Think about it.
I-- I went out.
And I thought about it.
And when I went in, I said, goodbye.
And I walked out just like that.
And I walked out.
And I found myself another job.
And that was a good one.
I went to-- to an architect's office.
No.
I-- I went to an architect's office.
And there I was the head of the-- I was the office manager.
And I did contracts and everything.
And then from there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to a chemical company.
By that time, I was a full-fledged accountant.
I became the controller for International Chemical Importer.
It was about 1970, something.
And I was there until I retired.
And then they were taking-- I was making a lot of money.
And I had a very good job.
And I was running the business all by myself.
We grew from a-- from a $1, $2-million business a year, to an $8 to $12 million.
And I had it all in my head.
And I felt wonderful.
And I did it wonderfully.
And then we were brought-- we were bought out by a Swedish-- one of our Swedish suppliers.
Because we were making a lot of money in the chemical business, industrial chemicals.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they wanted me to go on a computer.
And I said, I'm not a monkey.
I don't push buttons.
I didn't want to go on a computer.
I didn't want to feed a computer.
So they-- they gave me a golden parachute.
They threw me out.
They paid me for a year, and they threw me out.
And I wasn't old enough to get Social Security.
So I cried.
And my husband said-- I had married in-- I had married again, in 19-- we were together since 1980.
What's his name?
His name is Hyman Beizer.
And he's a very nice man.
And I made a much-- I knew much better what to look for the second time around.
And--
Yes, of course.
Well, my brother worked for Sperry Gyroscope.
And he was working on the Mariner spacecraft.
He was doing some technical writing.
And he was an electronics engineer.
He worked for-- for-- so he-- so they-- anyway, 1967, 1966, they came to recruit him for Israeli Aircraft Industries.
They paid for his-- they paid for his-- for his thing.
They came to recruit him.
They gave him a three-year contract.
And if he wanted to, after three years, they would send him back.
Well, he knew the moment that he picked up-- he had married a girl that was brought up in Israel.
She was a survivor.
Her parents had given her away when she was 10 months old.
They had a jewelry store in Berlin.
And she-- she was given to somebody in Belgium.
And after the war was over, and her parents didn't come back, they sent her to a-- a Youth Aliyah camp in Israel, where she was brought up.
And she had come here.
And my brother married her.
And they decided they were going to go back to Israel.
They had two children by that time.
And in 1960-- '69, they went to-- in 1967, they recruited him.
By 1969, everything was-- and they-- they went over.
And I-- I had never been apart from him.
And he was all I had left.
My father had died by then.
My father died in 1957, five months after my son was born.
He never saw the second one.
And he wanted so-- he was cute.
He used to say, I have a grandchild-- when Gregory was born-- you will not believe it.
He is a genius.
He is a child.
He's four weeks old.
You can talk to him about the stock market, about politics, anything.
He is wonderful.
He doesn't answer you.
But you can talk to him.
That was my father.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
Yeah, in Israel.
So when my-- so my brother was there.
I felt I had a reason to go there.
I have never felt-- I have never felt any very strong reason to go there.
I had an aunt there.
But it was very, very expensive.
And it was-- it was very far away.
And I was not connected to it.
One day, I would probably go.
But right now, I was busy building my life, having my children, putting my life together.
Then my marriage broke up.
And-- and-- and I was all alone for 12 years.
And I had three jobs.
Because I wanted to send my kids to private school.
And just because their father was a bastard, I didn't want them to have to suffer.
So they got what they-- what was coming to them.
And in the summer, they went to camp.
And I had to do that.
And that's what I did, for 12 years.
So now, was-- you know, so my brother went away.
And I said, OK, I'm going.
I'm-- I'm-- I have-- I have-- so the children went to camp in the summer.
I had some time.
And I went to Israel.
And I got off the plane, and it was the strangest thing.
The-- the-- I don't know where it came from, just, it-- it seemed like I was home.
I had never been there.
It was a strange country with a strange climate.
I didn't even know whether I would be able to drink the water.
They spoke a language which I remembered somewhere, way in back of my head.
But I didn't-- I wasn't into being Jewish.
I had seen too many bad things happening from one Jew to another.
I wasn't into being Jewish.
I wasn't interested.
But by that time, I had become Jewish again, because they were heroes.
So Israel, I was going to give them a chance.
I wouldn't have gone to a town in Poland.
Because I wasn't-- I wasn't able to deal with that, with lambs going to the slaughter and saying, yes sir.
Thank you sir.
I resented that.
And I didn't-- I didn't want to have anything to do with it.
So I went and-- and everything, it was very funny, when I came there, with the-- with the reading, and like that.
So bu I was there.
And I fell in love with the place.
And it spoke to me.
And I couldn't understand what anybody-- what anybody-- did I tell you about the Shakespearean?
Mhm.
Yeah, [? yeah, there-- ?] so what anybody said.
So I came home, and I went to the ulpan.
And the next time I went, I could understand a little bit.
And every chance I had, every single summer, year after year after year, except for 1978, when my daughter graduated from high school and I promised her a trip to California.
And we went for three week-- three weeks, all north, south, through California.
And I showed her the world.
I don't know, 23, 24 times.
I'm going home.
I'm going over there.
I remember my boss saying that-- when I was still working-- that-- why don't you-- he says, you have the chance to go.
I said, I'm going because that's where I want to be.
And he was saying something.
I says if I can't live that one month, I don't want to work the other 11, because it's not worth it.
Because unless I can buy myself something that I want for that money, I don't need to make it.
By that time, the children were going-- you know, it was getting later.
The children were going to school.
And I wasn't as-- as-- and I was making more money.
And I developed a bigger mouth.
I was more sure of myself.
Anyway, I went there.
And I have been going religiously back ever since, every single year, until I picked up with Hyman.
After the first three, four years, he said he wants to see the world.
We went to other places.
So we made a bargain, every other year.
So every other year, I go for six, seven weeks.
But in between, if there is a-- a wedding or something, I go the year that we go someplace else, I take another couple of weeks extra and I go to Israel.
I feel--
What it means is number one, when I came out of the war, I found that many people had had far worse-- what I considered worse-- experience than my own.
I always-- I think what happened to me is a nightmare.
It's worse than a nightmare.
It's-- it's something that's unimaginable among sane people.
But when I heard some of the stories that other people talked about, gas chambers and mountains of people, and mountains of hair, and-- I felt like I wasn't tattooed.
I felt like I didn't qualify to be-- to consider myself a member of the-- of the club.
So I felt all the years, I have to pay a price.
Why was I chosen?
What did I?
I didn't even pray to God.
Why was I chosen?
Why-- why-- why was I survive?
So I felt I owed all the people.
And first of all, I had to tell every-- I'm sorry.
I made a boo boo again.
I had to tell everybody about it.
And I had to publicize it.
I told my children.
I told anybody that was willing to listen.
But nobody was really interested.
It became fashionable, all of a sudden, when the 50th anniversary was approaching.
All of a sudden, everybody became conscious-- conscious what had happened 50 years before.
Before that, you couldn't sell it.
You couldn't give it away.
Anyway, I felt that I needed to tell it.
And I also felt that every time there was a-- a movie or a get together, or a story or something, I had to see it.
I had to subject myself.
And when-- when-- when my kids said, this is crazy.
You're tearing, you're sitting there, you're jumping.
You're-- you're going like that.
You don't sleep for two nights?
I said, that's-- that's the price I have to pay for being allowed to breathe.
I felt this very, very strongly.
And I still feel it, but to a lesser degree.
Because I decided-- I went for psychological counseling a year ago.
Because I was-- I was getting more and more angry, and more and more immersed in-- in-- in resentment.
And-- and it was interfering-- it wasn't interfering with my life.
I was unhappy.
And I was unhappy about being unhappy.
So I went.
And one day, the-- she said something to me that made a lot of sense.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, she wanted to know how I-- how I-- you know, that I hate.
I said, I don't hate.
I just don't want to have any part of it.
Because our people, underneath, if they're not Jewish and you scratch them, there's an anti-Semite underneath.
That's what I have found.
And if I'm wrong, so I make a mistake.
It doesn't make any difference.
And I-- I begin to resent people.
And I can't live that way.
I want to get myself out of that.
So she says, what about your children?
She says, do you hate your children?
I says, what a ridiculous question.
Of course I don't hate my children.
First of all, they never did anything to me.
And how could I possibly hate my children?
I adore them.
I would give my life for them.
She says, that means you control your emotions.
You controls whom you like and hate.
I says, but how can I-- She says, but you're in control of that.
You decide what is worth well, for you to love.
And all of a sudden, I went home.
And I said, wait a minute.
She's right.
I'm in control of this situation.
And this is 50 years later.
And I'm still a victim.
But nobody's making me a victim anymore.
I was a victim when somebody vandalized my father's gravestone.
I was a victim when-- when-- when-- when-- when somebody told me that if you wouldn't be Jewish, and you wouldn't be a woman, I would have to pay you $25,000 more a year.
But I could tell him what he could-- you know?
I could stand up.
I could talk back.
And if I didn't like it, I could walk away.
The only reason I am a victim is because I decide I'm still a victim.
And I was going to stop it.
And I managed.
I didn't manage to stop it.
But I managed to cut it down by at least 95%.
And it was like somebody had given me back the rest of my life.
I don't understand why I did it so long, and why it took me so much.
And why, all of a sudden, it's impossible.
And before it never did.
Maybe I had just outgrown it.
I don't know.
But it feels good not to be-- feel guilty, not to have been on the bottom of the pile.
What it feels like, first of all, it is a great relief.
Because I feel very strongly that everybody should know.
And that it is very unfortunate that some people are coming around.
Some of us-- I am one of the-- I am one of the youngest ones that were-- that were coherent, that know how to tell it, well and logically.
And I'm 70 years old.
I just turned 70.
I don't believe it.
But it happened.
This-- this is totally in-- I can't deal with it.
Anyway, so nobody will be here.
So I feel it is important.
I feel this is-- this to me is a gift, that I was privileged to be able to record it, and to tell it as it was, and to be able to say it as it is.
And that also-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I was very much afraid of this.
Because I thought that I was going to be hysterical.
And that I wasn't going to be-- I was afraid I was going to have nervous tics and-- and all kinds of things.
And I felt-- I feel very good about it.
I was able not to be logical and to be-- dispassionate is an overstatement.
But that I was able not to fold under it, under the pressure.
Because this is-- I-- I cannot talk about something that happened to me without going, that's the way I react, unfortunately.
I live through it, as I tell about it.
I feel it.
And the enormity of it frightens me, 24 hours a day.
I think about it all the time.
I have an obsession with food.
I think about food 24 hours a day.
I cannot eat any.
I know it will kill me if I eat as much as I want to.
But I want to.
Because I was so hungry for so long, that I'll never, ever-- no matter how full I am-- will I ever be able to mentally accept the fact that I have enough.
I know I will never ever need for anything or want for anything.
I was able to make a life for myself, luckily, with the gifts that I guess, God gave me, to-- to make myself.
I'm not wealthy.
But I'm comfortable.
I'll never be poor.
But I will never able to believe that I will always have enough food.
For the first six or seven years of my marriage, we threw out no food.
Because to me, I had to know that there was enough food.
My husband always used to say, comes the revolution, for six months, we'll be the only well-fed people.
Because I filled up all the closets with food.
I couldn't buy enough.
I still buy too much sometimes.
But I have come down.
I have made myself.
I-- I think that it is a miracle how much the human mind is able to with-- withstand without becoming unmanageable, without falling apart.

And the most important thing that we-- any one of us can do is to make sure that the younger generation, our generation, everybody's younger generation, understands how far a little bit of closing your eyes can lead to.
If it doesn't happen to you, it's not as bad.
It happens to all of us.
And if this happens, the world will not be a fit place to live in.
So we must all be very, very, very careful to do everything that we can to make sure that this seed of hatred doesn't come to flower again, and destroys another generation.
I think that's what-- that what I would like-- if I would know what to do about it, that's what I would like my life's work to be.
But of course, I am totally powerless.
And I want to live my own life.
I have worked so hard like a dog, all my life.
I want to coast for the rest of it.
Thank you for listening.
It was a privilege to be allowed to tell it.
